About

A Builder
of Things That Last

I am an Advocate based in Kampala, Uganda — practising law, serving in public institutions, and building ventures that I believe matter. My work sits at the intersection of law, technology, governance, and culture.

I have represented Uganda in international law and parliamentary diplomacy, working across twelve nations and engaging institutions including the African Development Bank and the World Bank. Locally, I have contributed to Uganda’s legislative process and public service through legal and procedural work across government.

In 2023, I was selected as a fellow of the Huduma Leadership Fellowship at the LéO Africa Institute — a thought leadership programme for emerging public sector champions in Uganda, run in partnership with the Konrad Adenauer Stiftung. In 2024, I began a Post-Graduate programme in Artificial Intelligence for Leaders at the McCombs School of Business, University of Texas at Austin.

“Uganda — Africa — is not a problem. She is a green paradise. The only thing missing is her people choosing to believe it.”

Beyond the formal: I am a chess player, a cricket enthusiast, a mathematics lover, and a writer in progress. I believe a person who can move a piece on the board, read the pitch, and hold a pen is a more complete human being.

Guided by Christian faith. Grounded in the conviction that how you do anything is how you do everything.

KampalaAdvocateBuilder Public ServiceInternational Law EsportsIndigenous Sport AI for LeadersChess CricketFaithAfrica

This website is the singular authoritative record of my public life — the reference point for all other representations of Maurice Muhumuza across media and platforms.

☯

Chess & Mathematics

The discipline of the board and the precision of numbers run through everything else. Strategy is thinking several moves ahead — in the courtroom and on the board alike.

Cricket & Sport

A cricket enthusiast who believes sport is a mirror for character — patience, strategy, and the long game. The same qualities that make a good advocate make a good player.

Writing & Ideas

Creative writing, blogging, and reading are not pastimes — they are the other side of legal reasoning. Where argument ends, story begins. This journal is where that side lives.

Legal Research

Proficient in SAFLII, ULII, and CANLII — the continent’s leading legal research databases. Research is not a background skill; it is the foundation of every credible argument.

Journal

The Unedited
Record

This journal is not a blog. It is not curated, edited for public consumption, or written with an audience in mind. It is the daily record of a mind in motion — thoughts as they arrived, questions as they stood, conclusions left unfinished where they were unfinished. Every entry is dated and untouched.

5th October 2025  ·  Philosophy

Plato’s Republic,
Book I — Summarised

What is justice? What value is justice? Through the macro-lens, where shall we find it? What would a “City of Justice” look like?

The Greek word for Justice, “Dikaiosyne”, takes a broader form — to include spiritual alignment, moral aptitude, and good social standing in the realm of righteousness and duty.

Four definitions are tested and each broken. Speaking truth and paying debts. Giving each what is appropriate. Benefiting friends and harming enemies. And finally, Thrasymachus: justice as a construct of the powerful to rule the weak. Socrates refutes them all.

By refuting Thrasymachus, Socrates argues that justice must always be to every person’s benefit in society, not a minority powerful. In other words, if the tree falls in the middle of the forest, away from anyone, has it fallen? Yes it has.

What is justice to me, today, in Kampala?

5th October 2025 continued…  ·  Philosophy

The Consequences
of Justice

Book 2 – The Consequences of Justice in “The Beautiful City”

Glaucon and Adeimantus hold a dialogue about the consequences of justice. Glaucon argues that if Socrates is to frame justice as a good thing, he should prove why it is good in its own right, as well as the consequences of living a just life.

He proposes that justice is only good for its consequences. It is therefore important that society is not only just, but seen to be just.

Glaucon tells the story of the Gyges, a ring that can make you invisible. With the ring you can be unjust without facing the consequences of the injustice. The story concludes with the Lydian Shepherd, the first holder of the ring, misusing it to unjust effect; seducing the queen, killing the king and ruling in tyranny.

Socrates’ rebuttal is to the effect that justice should not be limited to the actions of an individual but expand to include the entirety of society; a “City of Justice”/ Kallipolis.

Foundationally, Socrates posits the principles of justice to include specialisation of labour, by which each person will do the thing they are most suited for, and by extension, creation of an army to protect the city; “The Guardians”.

Plato’s Republic — Book 2

5th October 2025 continued…  ·  Philosophy

Who Shall Guard
the Guardians?

Book 3 – Who shall guard the Guardians; “The Noble Lie”

When you have a powerful guardian class, who guards them?

The answer would be music to the disciples of Sigmund Freud; you identify and shape the Guardians from childhood when the soul is most malleable. Theatre (drama) is therefore a sore to the eye in the City of Justice, since they encapsulate all the vices to be avoided.

“It is impossible for a human to imitate that which the soul cannot absorb.” i.e. Mimesis.

“The noble lie” is the foundation of all stable societies. The noble lie categorises citizens in the city of justice, based entirely on meritocracy and not by hereditary right, into four categories; children are born with gold, silver, bronze or iron spoons in their mouths.

The Rulers are born with gold, the Guardians with silver, the craftsmen and farmers with iron and bronze. Under this arrangement, the children belong to the city of justice and not to the parents.

“We don’t tell stories of dragons because they exist, but because they can be defeated.” — Neil

Plato’s Republic — Book 3

5th October 2025 continued…  ·  Philosophy

The Tripartite
Soul

Book 4 – “The Noble Lie/myth” continued

While the Rulers are born with gold in their souls, they should be the poorest among the citizens, holding no property or wealth. This is because money corrupts rulers, and the City of Justice is not meant to make rulers happy, but rather create the best possible conditions for the citizens to flourish.

I suspect this line of thought is not quite popular among those born with gold spoons, were we to inquire of their opinion of the matter.

Socrates then pulls the principles of justice down to the human individual soul. The rational bit that should do all the ruling, the honour-loving part that should be courageous, and the appetitive part that ensures you have all the necessities for life.

Justice is a complex system in which each component does very well specifically the task it was naturally suited to do.

The four cardinal virtues; courage, wisdom, temperament and justice cover all other virtues. Moderation ensures that all parts of the city agree who is in charge, while justice ensures each part fulfils their roles.

Plato’s Republic — Book 4

5th October 2025 continued…  ·  Philosophy

Family vs State:
The Fate of the Guardian

Book 5 – Family Vs State; The Fate of the Guardian

Plato posits three waves; Man and Woman are to play equal parts and roles in the running of the City of Justice, based on merit.

Plato also suggests that children who shall grow to become Guardians belong to the entire city as their parents.

Where the elite (philosophers) conflict with the ruling class, chaos reigns in the city. Therefore, kings/rulers must take on some sort of philosophy/ideological undertaking.

On mating/dating/marrying, one must choose the best possible companion to give birth to the best possible children. Sounds like the foundation of eugenics, but that is a story for another day.

Plato observed the fall of the Republic as precipitated by two things; Dynasties, and the powerful becoming too wealthy. Only the brave in war, who love music and poetry and have attained some sort of human excellence should be considered for ruling.

Books 5 poses serious questions regarding the burden that comes with true leadership. It is a life dedicated to understanding and leading others, first by leading oneself. It can be faked, but hardly sustained in that manner. As I lead towards understanding the theological foundations of Christianity, I think Plato’s Republic is, surprisingly, a great place to start.

Plato’s Republic — Book 5

6th October 2025  ·  Philosophy

Philosophers as Kings
& the Divided Line

Book 6 – Philosophers as Kings and the “Divided Line”

Plato reiterates the third wave; The ruling class should take on the tasks of learning, hate falsehood and should not be cowardly.

Plato notes that most societies will not let the good philosophers rule, rather opting for the skilled, because the philosopher seems useless, like a captain to a ship, who holds no specific role other than guide, but holds the most important role of them all.

They dedicate their lives to learning the form of the good. The form of good is imperceptible and is where every good thing springs from. It is abstract and can be likened to the sun which brings life and also allows us to see other things brought to life.

The Divided Line is an illustration of the progress of the lesser kinds of cognition (images/eikasia) to pistis, to concepts and finally to the perfect form (noesis/intellectual form). The philosopher’s journey is one to the mastery of the form.

Plato’s Republic — Book 6

5th August 2025  ·  Philosophy

Burying the Dead,
Watering the Living

I don’t want my thoughts to weigh heavily on me, as though they are made of matter. I dare to ask myself nonetheless, a bold, somewhat comic-sounding question. The kind a toddler asks their father.

“Are thoughts alive or dead?”

Before I express a thought, is it real? Has the tree fallen if no one saw it fall? Has my mind thought, if the thought was never projected to the world through words?

This brings me to my admiration of Egyptian, and Christian, eschatology. The Egyptian Book of Death is, unironically, a book about life, treating our earthly experiences as preparatory ceremonies for a more permanent life; that which we call life after death.

And yet, I pose the question once more, what do we know about life and death if we cannot ascertain the life of thoughts? Are they simply neurological electro-magnetic patterns that spring forth waves of electrons? Do we speak out that pattern with our voice, simply and nothing more?

What then do we say of lives unlived, yet resident within our thoughts? Are they worth funeral rites, as lives lived? I think they do. They too deserve to be buried and forgotten, respectfully.

To burying the dead and watering the living.

Amen.

8th August 2025  ·  Identity

Who Am I?
What Do I Want?

Who am I? I am that which I am, with or without presence of mind. The sum total of my past, aggregated, to the extent of my will and the present, and multi-tiered. I am who I think I am, and the validation of society certified through my actions, thoughts and words.

Who am I really? I am whosoever I choose to be, and the consequences that arrive through this choice in the world, to the extent of my interaction with the world.

Ok then; Suppose that is who I am, what wants spring forth from that person? Hopefully a meaningful life. To say, in silence and away from any human, “I lived and when I forget to, at least I wasted my life away to the benefit of others.”

In other words, I hope the affairs of being are not relegated a tier below the affairs of doing and speaking. I hope I can simply be and then let the world interact with me at the pace of the present. I also wish for a great partner, children and community.

Amen.

27th August 2025  ·  Self-Inquiry

My Limiting
Beliefs

It is interesting to notice that while anxiety itself creeps in un-announced, it dresses in whichever prevailing pressures one permits at any given time. As a boy, I was mostly free of anxiety as I found joy in the ordinary, like a sailor with no compass.

That anxiety is simply signal for the more ultimate question, “What am I afraid may happen and what can I do to avoid it happening?” began when a fellow child convinced me that I should be afraid of the dark. I remember that conversation vividly because suddenly, I knew there were unknown dangers in the world. Probably for my own good, God instilled that software at that time.

That being said, “Who am I?” as a question gets blurred by the anxiety, which crystallise into limiting beliefs.

“I cannot save money.”

How do I survive a limiting belief such as that above? Identify it. Understand how beneficial it was. Limit future ones. Remember it’s all a beautiful life to be experienced at a slow pace, not a race to a non-existent finish line. Unless in physical danger, limiting beliefs are abstract and can be deconstructed.

Amen.

26th August 2025  ·  Presence

Here.
Now.

I ask again. Who am I? What do I want? I am all the here and now, as a manifestation of every here and now before, and a foretelling of all the here and now in all incidences proceeding.

Perhaps in asking “Who am I?” in the same breath with “What do I want?” reveals the two tier step to accepting life itself, and living it through the acceptance.

Harmonising the song in my head telling me who I am. Wordless and yet deeply certain of the truth.

How do I harmonise “being” and “wanting”? By getting lost in the here and now, that being and wanting become an unquestionable clear component of who I am here, here and now.

Here and now are the livestreaming of reality and all its possibilities and reducing it to the only conceivable component of the future one wishes to experience as the here and now in future live streams. It’s not too much work, as it requires ignoring the past.

Amen.

3rd September 2025  ·  Morality

Parental Love
vs Kantian Morality

I look back with the wonder of a revelation ready for its time, and yet still aware that it is not yet ready for execution.

The greatest lie to rule them all is the lie told by society and more specifically by parents and those in authority, thereby shaping who one turns out to be in a Freudian sense, until fate and destiny play their part.

I was raised to believe that leadership was bestowed upon me as of inherent duty and that any past mediocrity in my family was a historical anomaly. Such was the lie told by society that Kant would condemn them in his school of absolute morality.

I often wonder if society does this deliberately in a crowd instinctive sense; a pre-natal lie. That being said, I remain myself, more so because of a mother’s love and society’s support.

Amen.

Ventures

What I’m
Building

01

Cheza Kwepena Federation

Preserving and professionalising Kwepena — an indigenous Ugandan sport — through governance, national festivals, and community-rooted development. A bet on Uganda’s own cultural identity.

kwepena.com ↗ 02

Nondescript Gaming Africa

Building Africa’s esports infrastructure from the ground up — competitive play, strategic partnerships, and the conviction that African youth deserve a world-class digital stage.

nondescriptgamingafrica.com ↗ 03

iStartApp

A technology venture built on the belief that African entrepreneurs deserve tools designed for their reality — not borrowed ones from elsewhere.

istartapp.xyz ↗
